articles.title: Cebu heat index may reach 40°C on Saturday
articles.description: LAPU-LAPU CITY, Cebu — Generally fair weather will continue in Cebu on Saturday, April 25, but hot and humid conditions may push the heat index up to 40 degrees Celsius, the state weather bureau said. In its daily forecast, the Visayas Pagasa Regional Services Division reported that actual temperatures in the province may range from 26 to 32 degrees Celsius. The heat index, however, may climb as high as 40 degrees Celsius, which falls under the “extreme caution” category. READ: Cebu heat index reaches 37°C on Friday; rain likely by late weekend The bureau explained that the heat index measures [...]... Keep on reading: Cebu heat index may reach 40°C on Saturday

articles.title: Violent tornado tears through Oklahoma town, destroys homes
articles.description: ENID, Okla. (AP) — Raeann Hunt scrambled to her cellar as a tornado bore down on her Oklahoma community. “It is headed right for us,” she recalled thinking, as she peeked outside, unable to contain her curiosity. articles.title: Everything but the kitchen sink: how to choose more sustainable and durable cookware
articles.description: What to donate, buy and borrow to free up space, use fewer resources and give your kitchenware a great shelf life Change by degrees offers life hacks and sustainable living tips each Saturday to help reduce your household’s carbon footprint Got a question or tip for reducing household emissions? Email us at changebydegrees@theguardian.com As a baby boomer and grandmother, I’m keen to find ways in my kitchen to try to make up for the damage wrought by my generation and help keep the planet sustainable for my children and grandchildren. As a former professional cook, I have the culinary tools to feed my growing family but am only now acquiring the knowledge to do so in a sustainable way by making small changes at minimal or no cost. articles.title: Brunei Darussalam: Brunei: Disaster Management Reference Handbook (April 2026)
articles.description: Country: Brunei Darussalam Source: Center for Excellence in Disaster Management and Humanitarian Assistance Please refer to the attached file. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Brunei Darussalam (hereafter “Brunei”) is a small, high-income, absolute monarchy on the northwest coast of Borneo Island in Southeast Asia. Its centralized governance model, substantial fiscal resources, and well-institutionalized disaster management framework enable effective responses to the country’s primary hazards – flooding and landslides. Brunei’s strong domestic response capacity and low overall disaster risk mean that international assistance is rarely required domestically. The most frequent hazard is flooding, driven by the Northeast Monsoon from December through March. Recurring flood impacts include temporary displacement of residents, road closures along critical arteries, damage to homes, disruption to water and drainage infrastructure, and restricted emergency access. Landslides, often triggered by prolonged rainfall, can damage critical infrastructure and lead to disruptions to water supply, health care, education, and business. Although the direct impacts of individual floods or landslides may be minimal, their cumulative impact nationally and over time can be considerable and begin to erode coping capacity. Brunei rarely requires international assistance after hazard events, as most incidents are managed effectively through national and district-level resources. There is a wellinstitutionalized disaster management system anchored by the Disaster Management Order of 2006, which established the National Disaster Council (NDC) and the National Disaster Management Centre (NDMC). The system follows a structured three-tiered response model, escalating from District Disaster Management Committees (DDMC) at the local level, through national coordination by the NDMC, to international assistance, when required. The Royal Brunei Armed Forces (RBAF), Royal Brunei Police Force (RBPF), and Brunei Fire and Rescue Department (BFRD) provide critical operational support, while the NDMC coordinates whole-of-government response efforts. The RBAF plays a critical supporting role in domestic and regional disaster response operations within a civilian-led national response framework. Civil-Military Cooperation Platoons within RBAF battalions are frequently deployed for on-the-ground assistance, including transportation, logistics, engineering support, and debris clearance. The three-tiered disaster management system provides a structured escalation pathway from local to international response. If district and national resources prove insufficient, Tier 3 activates international assistance, mostly via the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). Brunei is an active and committed participant in ASEAN’s disaster management frameworks, including the ASEAN Agreement on Disaster Management and Emergency Response (AADMER) and the “One ASEAN, One Response” policy. The NDMC serves as Brunei’s national focal point for ASEAN disaster management communication and coordination. Brunei works with other member states through the ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance on disaster management (AHA Centre) and use the ASEAN Standard Operating Procedure for Regional Standby Arrangements and Coordination of Joint Disaster Relief and Emergency Response Operations (SASOP). Brunei Darussalam presents a unique disaster management context characterized by low overall risk to large-scale disasters, strong domestic response capacity, and active regional engagement. However, structural vulnerabilities, including geographic discontinuity, economic dependence on hydrocarbons, food import reliance, and the needs of marginalized populations, require ongoing attention from planners and policymakers. This handbook provides the foundational knowledge necessary to support effective planning, coordination, and engagement with Brunei’s disaster management system.
